title: Coccidiosis in kits: recognition and treatment
subtitle: The protozoan parasite that kills young rabbits between weeks three and six.
excerpt: Coccidiosis caused by Eimeria species is the most common cause of digestive disease and death in young rabbits between weaning and 8 weeks of age. Most adult rabbits carry the oocysts without clinical signs; kits are susceptible in the weeks when immunity is developing and gut flora is establishing. Recognising and treating early saves a litter.
sourceNotes: Merck Veterinary Manual, coccidiosis in rabbits (online edition). RWAF disease information, rwaf.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: Disease caused by Eimeria protozoan parasites that complete their life cycle in the intestinal lining (or liver, in hepatic coccidiosis). Transmitted via faecal-oral route. Young rabbits in the 3 to 8 week age window are most vulnerable to lethal intestinal forms.
The environmentally resistant egg-like stage of coccidian parasites passed in faeces. Survives weeks in damp bedding; killed by drying, direct sunlight, or steam cleaning.

content.text: in kits takes two main forms: intestinal (gut, diarrhoea, rapid death) and hepatic (liver, weight loss, jaundice). The intestinal form is more acute and more often fatal. It is the main killer in kits from weaning to 8 weeks old.
Watery or mucoid diarrhoea (normal rabbit faeces are firm dry pellets)
Rapid loss of condition and weight in the 3 to 6 week old age range
Hunched posture, reduced activity, and failure to compete at the feeder
Deaths occurring in multiple kits within the same litter or across litters sharing a pen

Sulphonamide drugs in drinking water are the standard treatment, available from a vet. Start early. A kit that is already dehydrated and weak has little chance. Give electrolyte solution alongside the drug to replace fluids. Complete the full course even if signs clear early. Stopping early increases the risk of a repeat episode.

survive in damp bedding for weeks and are resistant to standard chemical disinfectants. The most effective environmental control is drying and direct sunlight, or steam cleaning at temperatures above 56°C. Remove and replace all bedding at the start of an outbreak, and keep the pen dry. Feed pellets in raised feeders rather than off the ground to reduce faecal-oral contamination of feed.

Keep does' housing clean before kindling; kits acquire their first coccidia exposure from the doe. Reduce stocking density at weaning, crowding accelerates transmission and immune stress. Gradual weaning (removing the doe for increasing periods rather than all at once) reduces the stress-induced immune dip that triggers susceptibility.
